1: The covenant ratification ceremony (Exodus 24) is one of the most significant events in the OT: the formal establishment of the Sinai covenant between YHWH and Israel. The four-tier structure of who may approach — Moses alone at the summit, Aaron and Nadab and Abihu at a middle level, the seventy elders lower, and the people at the base — reflects the graduated holiness structure of sacred space that will be built into the tabernacle's design (most holy place → holy place → outer court → camp). The seventy elders (representing the full body of Israel; cf. the 70 nations in Gen 10) are included in the vision but at a subordinate level.
2: 'Moses alone is to approach the LORD; the others must not come near' — the exclusive mediation of Moses at the summit structures the covenant: YHWH speaks to Israel through a single authorized mediator. This sets the pattern for all subsequent Israelite prophecy (YHWH speaks through a prophet, not directly to the community) and anticipates the NT's category of Jesus as the unique mediator (1 Timothy 2:5: 'there is one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus'). The graduated approach — elders nearer than people, Moses nearer than elders — reflects the incarnational logic: those who bear more responsibility come closer.
3: 'The people responded with one voice: “Everything the LORD has said we will do”' — the unanimous covenant acceptance is the formal response that constitutes Israel as a covenant community. In ANE suzerain-vassal treaties, the vassal's formal acceptance of the treaty terms created the binding covenant relationship. The phrase 'all the words which the LORD has spoken' (kol-hadĕbārîm ʾăšer dibber YHWH) refers to the Decalogue and the Book of the Covenant (Exod 20-23). Israel's triple consent — here, after the blood-sprinkling (Exodus 24:7), and again (Exodus 24:8) — shows the ceremony is structured to make the commitment irrevocable and communally witnessed.
4: 'Moses then wrote down everything the LORD had said' — this is the first explicit reference to Moses as a writer in the Pentateuch, and the written document ('the Book of the Covenant') is immediately incorporated into the ratification ceremony. The writing creates a permanent, authoritative record that can be read publicly (Exodus 24:7). ANE suzerain-vassal treaties always included a written document deposited in the sanctuary of the deities and read publicly at regular intervals (Deuteronomy 31:10-11 commands a seven-year public reading of the Torah). The twelve pillars Moses erects represent the twelve tribes as witnesses to the covenant — a standard ANE treaty feature.
5: Young men (naʿărê bĕnê yiśrāʾēl) offer the burnt offerings and peace offerings — before the Levitical priesthood is established (Exod 28-29; Lev 8-9), sacrifices are offered by designated laymen from the community. This confirms that the Levitical priesthood is not a creation-order institution but a covenant-specific institution established at Sinai. The peace offerings (šĕlāmîm) — whose blood will be used in the covenant ratification — are distinct from the burnt offerings: they involve a shared meal between the offerer, the priest, and YHWH (symbolically), making them the natural sacrifice for covenant-sealing.
6: Moses divides the blood into two halves: half against the altar (YHWH's side of the covenant) and half reserved for the people (Exodus 24:8). This binary division is unique in OT ritual and mirrors the covenant structure: YHWH and Israel are the two parties, and the blood bonds both. The altar represents YHWH; the people represent Israel. The blood that bridges them is the covenant's sealing medium — the life of the sacrifice stands in for the lives of the parties who are bound by death-oath. If either party breaks the covenant, they call down on themselves what happened to the sacrifice.
7: 'He took the Book of the Covenant and read it to the people. They responded, “We will do everything the LORD has said; we will obey”' (naʿăśeh wĕnišmāʿ) — the Hebrew word order is significant: 'we will do and we will hear/obey.' The Talmud (Shabbat 88a) treats this as Israel's greatest moment: they committed to do before they had fully heard all the implications, a contrast to normal human contract negotiation. The public reading of the covenant document before the people's formal acceptance mirrors ANE treaty practice: the vassal must be informed before consenting. The people's response makes them personally responsible for what was read.
8: 'This is the blood of the covenant that the LORD has made with you' — Moses's words at the blood-sprinkling are the most directly quoted OT text in the NT's eucharistic tradition. Jesus at the Last Supper says: 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many' (Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24) — an unmistakable echo of Moses's words in Exodus 24:8. Hebrews 9:18-20 cites the Sinai blood-sprinkling as the prototype of covenant inauguration and argues that the new covenant's ratification through Christ's blood is the same pattern carried to its ultimate fulfillment. The covenant is not complete until blood seals it.
9: The seventy elders (plus Moses, Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu — seventy-four people) ascend the mountain for the theophanic meal. The number seventy recurs throughout the OT as the representative total of Israel: the seventy descendants who went down to Egypt (Genesis 46:27), the seventy nations of Gen 10, the seventy elders appointed by Moses (Numbers 11:16-25), and Jesus's sending of seventy disciples (Luke 10:1). The seventy elders represent the entire covenant community; their vision of God on the mountain is Israel's representative encounter with the divine presence.
10: 'They saw the God of Israel. Under his feet was something like a pavement made of lapis lazuli, as bright as the sky itself' — this is one of the most audacious theophanies in Scripture. The elders see YHWH (though the focus falls on 'under his feet' rather than his full form, consistent with the tradition that the full divine face cannot be seen by mortals, Exodus 33:20). Lapis lazuli (sappîr) — the vivid blue semi-precious stone mined in Afghanistan and traded throughout the ancient Near East — was associated with the sky and with royal/divine magnificence. Ezekiel's vision of the divine throne (Ezekiel 1:26; Exodus 10:1) also features a sapphire pavement, directly recalling this text.
11: 'But God did not raise his hand against these leaders of the Israelites; they saw God, and they ate and drank' — the eating and drinking in YHWH's presence on the mountain is the covenant meal, sealing the treaty relationship. In ANE treaty practice, the covenant meal shared between the suzerain and vassal ratified the relationship — it was not merely celebratory but constitutive. The fact that YHWH 'did not raise his hand' is the narrator's way of emphasizing the extraordinary grace of this encounter: the humans expected to die from divine exposure (Exodus 33:20; Judges 13:22) but instead ate in YHWH's presence. This is the prototype of the eschatological banquet (Isaiah 25:6-8; Revelation 19:9).
12: 'Come up to me on the mountain and stay here, and I will give you the tablets of stone with the law and commandments I have written for their instruction' — Moses is summoned to receive the stone tablets of the Decalogue (later given in Exodus 31:18). The tablets are described as written by YHWH himself (Exodus 31:18: 'written by the finger of God'), uniquely distinguishing the Decalogue from the Book of the Covenant (which Moses wrote, Exodus 24:4). The divine inscribing of the Decalogue signals its foundational status within the covenant — it is not merely the report of a human mediator but the direct expression of YHWH's own authority.
13: Joshua is introduced here as Moses's aide (mĕšārēt, servant/minister) — the term used for Elisha's service to Elijah and for priestly service at the sanctuary. Joshua's presence with Moses partway up the mountain (he apparently waits while Moses continues alone) is the first indication of his role as Moses's successor. The name Yĕhôšûaʿ (Joshua) means 'YHWH saves' — the same root as Yēšûaʿ (Jesus). The coincidence is noted in Hebrews 4:8: Joshua could not give the people their ultimate rest; only Jesus can.
14: 'Aaron and Hur are with you. Anyone involved in a dispute can go to them' — Aaron and Hur function as the governing council in Moses's forty-day absence. The two men represent civil (Hur, the military leader of Exodus 17:10) and religious (Aaron, the high priest designate) authority. Their joint leadership during Moses's absence is a pattern for shared governance. The failure they experience — Aaron's capitulation to the golden calf demand (Exodus 32) — will be the defining failure of the transitional period, showing that the people require the ongoing presence of the singular mediator Moses rather than divided leadership.
15: The cloud (ʿānān) that covers Sinai when Moses ascends is the same pillar of cloud that has guided Israel through the wilderness — the cloud is the visible manifestation of the glory (kābôd) of YHWH. The cloud-cover serves as a veil: the divine glory is present but not immediately visible to the people below. Six days of waiting precede Moses's summons on the seventh day — a pattern mirroring creation (six days + seventh day of divine rest) and anticipating the tabernacle construction where the cloud-glory will descend on the completed sanctuary (Exodus 40:34-35).
16: 'The glory of the LORD settled (wayyiškōn) on Mount Sinai' — the verb šākan (to dwell, to tabernacle) is the root of the noun miškan (tabernacle, dwelling) and the later Jewish theological concept of the šĕkînāh (divine presence). YHWH's glory 'tabernacling' on Sinai anticipates the tabernacle that will be built to house that same glory. John 1:14 uses the same word: 'the Word became flesh and tabernacled (ἐσκήνωσεν) among us, and we have seen his glory.' The Sinai theophany is thus the proto-tabernacle: the glory that will permanently dwell in the sanctuary first rests on the mountain.
17: 'To the Israelites the glory of the LORD looked like a consuming fire on top of the mountain' — the fire theophany (also Exodus 3:2; Exodus 19:18) is the standard Sinai image in later OT memory: Deuteronomy consistently describes Sinai as 'the mountain burning with fire' (Deuteronomy 4:11; Exodus 5:23; Exodus 9:15). Hebrews 12:18-24 uses this description as the foil for the new covenant's 'Mount Zion' — the terrifying mountain that cannot be touched (Sinai) contrasted with the joyful assembly of the firstborn (Zion). The consuming fire of divine glory is the same fire that will later consume the tabernacle sacrifices (Leviticus 9:24) and the NT's tongues of fire at Pentecost (Acts 2:3).
18: 'Moses entered the cloud as he went on up the mountain. And he stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights' — the forty-day period on Sinai is the paradigmatic period of intimate divine encounter. Moses's two forty-day sojourns on Sinai (this one and Exodus 34:28) frame the golden calf apostasy between them. Elijah's forty-day journey to Horeb (1 Kings 19:8) and Jesus's forty days of testing in the wilderness (Matthew 4:2) both deliberately echo the Moses-Sinai pattern. In OT typology, forty days or forty years marks a complete period of formation/testing before a new phase of redemptive history begins.
